title: Your First Sketch: Observational Drawing from Life
description: Practice observational drawing by sketching an ordinary object from direct observation. This session introduces contour drawing, teaches you to see rather than symbolize, and produces your first observational study.

steps.instruction: Choose a simple object with interesting edges: a shoe, a crumpled piece of paper, a hand, a plant. Place it in good light where you can see it clearly. Before drawing, read about observational drawing -- why is it important to draw what you SEE rather than what you KNOW?
steps.expectedOutcome: You understand that drawing from observation means suppressing the symbolic shorthand your brain uses for familiar objects
steps.hint: The goal is to draw the object you actually see, not your idea of what the object looks like

steps.instruction: Begin with a blind contour drawing: place your pencil at one edge of the object and move it very slowly along the paper WITHOUT looking at the paper, keeping your eye on the object's edges. Spend 3 minutes. Read about contour drawing technique.
steps.expectedOutcome: Your blind contour may look distorted but shows you attended to edge detail you would have ignored with a symbolic approach
steps.hint: Move the pencil at the same speed as your eye -- like a scanner tracing the edge. Never lift the pencil, and never look at the paper.

steps.instruction: Now do a 5-minute modified contour drawing: same slow, deliberate approach but you may glance at the paper occasionally to check relationships. Focus on proportion -- how large is one part relative to another? Read about gesture drawing to understand the difference between quick gesture and slow contour.
steps.expectedOutcome: Your modified contour shows improved accuracy compared to the blind contour while retaining the careful observation
steps.hint: When you glance at the paper, compare proportions -- is this part too large or too small relative to the reference?

steps.instruction: Do a 2-minute gesture sketch of the same object: this time, capture the overall shape and energy quickly, not the edges. Think of the gesture as the skeleton of the drawing. Read about composition principles -- what is the most important visual element of your object?
steps.expectedOutcome: You understand that gesture and contour are complementary -- gesture establishes the whole, contour develops the parts

steps.instruction: Review all three drawings. What changed? Which shows the most accurate observation? Write a brief note about what was hardest -- where did your symbolic knowledge override your observation?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can identify specific moments where your brain substituted a symbol for observation, and you have three studies showing different approaches to the same object
